common_name: Waltham Butternut Squash (Classic Long-Storing Winter Squash Heirloom)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer (July in zone 9) in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun for a fall harvest; Waltham Butternut is a Cucurbita moschata variety with better heat and humidity tolerance than pepo-type winter squash — an important practical advantage for Gulf South production.

directions: Direct sow in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Waltham Butternut is the standard commercial and home garden butternut squash — developed by Robert Young at the Waltham Experiment Station in Massachusetts and recognized with an All-America Selections award for its exceptional production, uniformity, and storage quality. The classic cylindrical fruits with tan skin, small seed cavity, and large neck of deep-orange flesh represent the butternut ideal: consistent shape, excellent flavor, and long storage life (3–6 months at cool room temperature). The Cucurbita moschata species provides better heat and humidity tolerance than C. pepo winter squash types — allowing Waltham Butternut to succeed in Gulf South conditions where pepo types (acorn, Delicata) often struggle with disease and heat in late-season production. In zone 9, the most reliable production method is a late-summer planting (July–August direct sow) for a fall harvest (October–November) — the fall planting avoids peak summer heat during vulnerable early growth and produces during the cooler fall weather that improves storage quality. Cure harvested fruits for 10 days at 80–85°F to harden the skin and develop the characteristic butterscotch sweetness that distinguishes fully cured butternut from freshly harvested green fruit. Waltham Butternut vines spread extensively (6–10 feet) and require substantial garden space.

common_name: Purple Ruffles Basil (AAS-Winning Ornamental Deep-Purple Frilled Basil)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; purple basil colors are most intense in full sun — partial shade produces greenish-purple foliage with less dramatic color.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Purple Ruffles is an All-America Selections gold medal-winning basil combining exceptional ornamental quality with full culinary utility — producing large, deeply ruffled, deeply frilled leaves in rich dark-purple to reddish-purple coloration that is immediately striking in the herb garden or as an ornamental annual. The large, heavily ruffled leaf margins — with deeply incised, wavy, almost lace-like edges — distinguish Purple Ruffles from other purple basils whose leaves are smooth or only slightly ruffled. The ornamental quality is outstanding: the combination of the vivid dark-purple color and elaborate leaf texture creates a decorative impact far beyond most culinary herbs. The basil flavor is good — a full, classic basil flavor with a slight spicy-anise character and a pleasant mild bitterness from the purple anthocyanins — suitable for pesto, salads, and infused oils where the purple color creates a striking visual effect (purple basil vinegar is particularly beautiful). In zone 9, purple basil grows through the warm season from April–October but often bolts (flowers) quickly in summer heat; pinch flower buds promptly and repeatedly to extend the harvest season. The purple leaf color makes excellent contrast with silver herbs (artemisia, horehound) and chartreuse-leaved companions in herb garden designs.

common_name: Italian Large Leaf Basil (Oversized Smooth-Leaf Classic Pesto Basil)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Italian Large Leaf basil bolts quickly in Gulf South summer heat — pinch flower stalks immediately and repeatedly to extend leaf production; succession sowings every 3 weeks from April through August ensure continuous fresh basil.

directions: Direct sow or transplant after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun; Italian Large Leaf basil produces very large (3–4 inch), smooth, flat, slightly cupped, glossy leaves with the classic sweet, aromatic, clove-and-anise basil flavor of traditional Italian cooking — the preferred basil for pesto, where the large leaf size dramatically reduces the time and effort required to harvest sufficient leaf mass for a batch. The large, smooth leaves with their flat, relatively unwrinkled surface represent a distinctly different basil character from small-leaf and ruffled types — they are easier to clean, process, and stack, and produce a cleaner pesto with less stem fiber. The vigorous plants reach 2–2.5 feet with regular harvesting that removes flower buds and promotes continued vegetative growth. In zone 9, Italian Large Leaf basil grows throughout the warm season (April–October) and succession planting every 3 weeks maintains continuous fresh supply despite the tendency of summer heat to promote bolting in individual plants. For maximum leaf harvest, remove flower stalks immediately when they appear — flowering redirects plant energy from leaf production and quickly reduces the quality and flavor of remaining leaves. A single-season basil capable of producing many pounds of leaves from a short row under good management.

common_name: Raven Zucchini (AAS-Winning Dark-Green Disease-Tolerant Compact Zucchini)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Raven's compact, open plant habit improves air circulation around the crown — a practical advantage in the humid Gulf South where powdery mildew is a consistent challenge for summer squash.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Raven is an All-America Selections-winning zucchini hybrid notable for its very dark green to nearly black-green skin color, compact open plant habit, excellent flavor, and improved disease tolerance. The extremely dark skin — darker than standard dark-green zucchini — is the most visually distinctive quality: Raven zucchini are nearly black-green at harvest size, creating a visually striking contrast with yellow and light-green squash in market displays and mixed vegetable presentations. The compact, open plant habit (non-vining, upright-spreading, with good air circulation through the plant canopy) reduces powdery mildew pressure in Gulf South humid conditions where powdery mildew often devastates standard bush-type zucchini by mid-season. The earlier maturity (48–55 days) compared to many other zucchini allows faster first harvest and more production within the spring window before summer heat. In zone 9, plant in February–March for spring production and again in August–September for fall production. Harvest Raven at 6–8 inches for best flavor and texture — the dark skin makes it slightly harder to judge size at a glance, so check plants frequently during peak production. An outstanding choice for Gulf South summer gardens where disease pressure is the primary limitation of zucchini production.

common_name: San Diego Red Bougainvillea (Vivid Scarlet-Red Large Vigorous Tropical Vine)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to average soil in full sun; San Diego Red is among the most cold-hardy bougainvillea cultivars — it survives brief temperature drops to 25–28°F with some dieback but typically returns from the base.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean soil in full sun; San Diego Red (also sold as 'Scarlett O'Hara' or 'Rosenka') is a large, vigorous bougainvillea cultivar known for its vivid scarlet-red to brilliant red bract color — one of the most intensely red bougainvilleas available, producing masses of brilliant red bracts that create a bold, high-impact tropical display when the vine is in full bloom. The bract color is described as more purely red (rather than the magenta-red of Barbara Karst) — a cleaner, more saturated crimson-to-scarlet tone that creates a different visual temperature in the landscape. San Diego Red is also noted as one of the more cold-hardy bougainvillea cultivars for zone 9 — surviving brief periods below freezing better than many other varieties, with some gardeners in zone 9a successfully maintaining it as a returning perennial with winter protection. Like all bougainvilleas, San Diego Red blooms most prolifically under slightly stressful conditions: periodic dry-down between waterings, lean soil without excessive nitrogen, and full sun exposure produce the heaviest flower display. The large, vigorous vine can reach 20+ feet on a wall or pergola structure in favorable conditions. Outstanding trained against a south or west-facing wall where reflected heat enhances bloom production and the structure protects the vine from cold north winds in zone 9 winters.
